blocks.content: He went to The Copper Mug after, because he went there most mornings after, and because right now the familiarity of it felt necessary — the specific smell of the place, the quality of the light, the way the room had its own weather that had nothing to do with whatever was happening outside it.
Mr. Alvarez set a coffee in front of him before he'd fully settled into the chair.
He sat. He looked at the coffee for a while without picking it up.

The morning moved around them — the grinder, a man at the counter ordering something complicated, the door opening and letting in a bar of light. Ethan sat inside all of it and was aware of none of it.

Mr. Alvarez waited until the early rush had passed. Then he came around from behind the counter, pulled out the chair across from Ethan — the particular chair — and sat down with the unhurried ease of a man who has made his peace with the pace of important things.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: I notice things. I know what you look like when you're working. I know what you look like when you're thinking. Right now you look like neither of those.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Like a man carrying something he cannot put down and cannot yet name.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Only to someone paying attention. I pay attention. It began as a professional habit. It has become a personal one.
